questions.questionText: A postmenopausal woman with a T-score of -3.0 on DEXA scan is diagnosed with:
questions.optionB: Osteoporosis (T-score ≤ -2.5 on DEXA = osteoporosis; between -1.0 and -2.5 = osteopenia; > -1.0 = normal)
questions.explanation: Core concept: WHO DEXA criteria: T-score ≤ -2.5 = osteoporosis. Why correct: DEXA (Dual-Energy X-ray Absorptiometry) at lumbar spine and hip. T-score compares patient's BMD to young healthy adult reference. T-score > -1.0 = normal; -1.0 to -2.5 = osteopenia; ≤ -2.5 = osteoporosis; ≤ -2.5 + fragility fracture = established/severe osteoporosis. Why A wrong: -3.0 is well below -1.0 threshold. Why C wrong: >-2.5 needed for osteopenia only. Why D wrong: Osteomalacia = defective mineralization (Looser zones, elevated ALP, low vitamin D). Clinical relevance: Treatment: bisphosphonates (alendronate/zoledronic acid) first-line + calcium + vitamin D. FRAX tool for fracture risk assessment. Complications: vertebral compression fractures, hip fractures, Colles fracture. Exam trap: T-score for postmenopausal women; Z-score for premenopausal women and children (compares to age-matched). Memory aid: 'T-score = Ten-year comparison (young reference); ≤-2.5 = osteoporosis.' Reference: Maheshwari – Metabolic Bone Disease.
